narrative_blocks.header: He moved west to Wisconsin in old age
narrative_blocks.body: Reuben Smith married Elizabeth Porter at Saratoga in 1825 and raised his family in New York. By 1860 he had moved to Beaver Dam, Wisconsin, where he died that November.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband oldest homeopathic physician on Pacific coast
narrative_blocks.body: Emily's husband Dr. Ziba Bass Nichols was described at his 1897 death as 'oldest practicing homeopathic physician on the Pacific coast' — a Dartmouth-trained physician who had worked the homeopathy revolution from its Minnesota mid-century days through to its Oregon-frontier late-century practice. He had served as physician and surgeon of the Minnesota state asylum for the deaf, dumb, and blind for eighteen years before the family's move to Portland.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband preceptor of the Mayo brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Nichols was described in a descendant biographical sketch as 'preceptor of the Mayo brothers' — placing the Porter-Nichols family in proximity to Charles Mayo and William Mayo, the founders of the Mayo Clinic. The preceptor-and-student relationship in nineteenth-century American medicine was a formal apprenticeship; if accurate, this placed Emily's household near the formative training of two of America's most consequential physicians.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons became Portland physicians
narrative_blocks.body: Three of Emily and Dr. Nichols's ten children became Portland physicians in turn: Dr. Ammi Sibley Nichols (1854-1931), Dr. Clarence Lincoln Nichols (1861-1911), and Dr. Herbert Strong Nichols (1868-1948). The three-physician second generation extended the family's medical-practice continuity from 1844 marriage to mid-twentieth-century Oregon care.

narrative_blocks.header: Worcester MA 1826 to Portland OR 1901
narrative_blocks.body: Emily Porter was born in 1826 in Worcester, Worcester County Massachusetts, daughter of Benjamin Porter and Clementina Lamb. She died September 11, 1901 in Portland Oregon at about age seventy-five and is buried at River View Cemetery, Portland. She had been a resident of Portland eighteen years prior to her death.

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Moses Porter' for grandfather Col. Moses Porter (HD2434).

institutions.institution_blurb: Licensed to preach in 1868 at Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church) — historic Black Congregational congregation founded 1826 in Hartford.

military_service.rank: Private (1862-64) → First Lieutenant and Brevet Captain (1864)
units: 39th United States Colored Troops (1864) — Black Union Army regiment
Aide-de-Camp to General Duncan, New Bern North Carolina (1865)
military_service.role: Enlisted Massachusetts state regiment 1862; transitioned to officer commission with one of the U.S. Colored Troops Black Union regiments in 1864. Served as aide-de-camp to General Duncan in New Bern North Carolina through war's end.

burial.plot_notes: Congressional Cemetery, Washington, District of Columbia. Cemetery linkage repair: existing person was listed in cemetery hooker_connections in prior pilots but burial.cemetery_id was null. Pilot104 closure of bidirectional reference.

narrative_blocks.header: USCT officer, Freedmen's Bureau Superintendent
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell rose from Private in the 36th Massachusetts Volunteers (1862-1864) to First Lieutenant and Brevet Captain in the 39th United States Colored Troops in 1864 — one of the Black Union Army regiments. He served as Aide-de-Camp to General Duncan in New Bern North Carolina in 1865. Immediately postwar he became Superintendent of the Freedmen's Bureau in Washington D.C., a significant Reconstruction-era role coordinating federal support for newly emancipated African Americans.

narrative_blocks.header: Licensed to preach at Talcott Street Church, 1868
narrative_blocks.body: After ordination on May 8, 1867 from Hartford Theological Seminary, Moses Porter Snell was licensed to preach in 1868 at Talcott Street Congregational Church in Hartford — the historic Black Congregational congregation that had been founded in 1826. The Talcott Street licensing parallel between his Reconstruction-era career and his ministerial commissioning at one of New England's most prominent Black churches is striking. He spent the next forty years as missionary and pastor in Washington D.C. and Maryland-Virginia churches.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson of Amherst College founder Thomas Snell
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell was grandson of Dr. Thomas Snell, founder of Amherst College, and nephew of Ebenezer S. Snell (Amherst class of 1822). He himself graduated Amherst in 1868 and attended Hartford Theological Seminary 1866-1868. The two-generation Amherst College connection — founder grandfather, alumnus uncle, alumnus himself — was characteristic of the New England Congregational scholarly families that dominated late-nineteenth-century mainline Protestant institutional leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Brookfield MA, buried Congressional Cemetery DC
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell was born May 3, 1839 in North Brookfield Massachusetts and died December 31, 1909 in Newton New Jersey at age seventy. He was buried at Congressional Cemetery in Washington D.C. He married Mary Cordelia Hallock on August 31, 1862 — the same year he enlisted in the 36th Massachusetts. They had nine children.

sources.label: Yale Biographical Sketches (Dexter), Vol. I — Eleazer Porter

narrative_blocks.header: JP at 30 — dual judge by 1779
narrative_blocks.body: He received his first commission as Justice of the Peace in 1758, at age thirty. In 1777 he was made Judge of the Court of Common Pleas and in 1779 Judge of Probate — both offices held until his death in 1797.

narrative_blocks.header: First wife was his first cousin
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Anna Pitkin was his first cousin through the Pitkin grandfather they shared. She died November 7, 1758 in her twenty-fourth year, leaving two daughters; two years later he married Susannah Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Married widower Eleazer Porter Jr. in 1760
narrative_blocks.body: In 1760 she married Eleazer Porter Jr. — Yale 1748, lawyer and judge in Hadley, Massachusetts. His first wife Anna Pitkin, his own first cousin, had died November 1758 leaving two young daughters; Susannah became their stepmother.

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother married her sister-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: Her brother Jonathan Edwards Jr. married her husband's sister Mary Porter in 1770. The two pairs of Edwards and Porter siblings married each other across the same Connecticut River Valley generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Son's marriage doubled the Edwards descent
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Jonathan Edwards Porter married Fidelia Dwight in the next generation. Both were grandchildren of Jonathan Edwards Sr. — a marriage that doubled the Edwards descent in their children's bloodline.

parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Stoddard — daughter of Rev. Solomon Stoddard of Northampton
parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Timothy Edwards of East Windsor CT — minister 60 years until death 1758 age 89

church_roles.notes: Succeeded grandfather Solomon Stoddard. Led Great Awakening revivals. Dismissed 1750 over communion controversy.

X00128.prominent_name_reason: Rev. Jonathan Edwards (1703-1758) — Yale 1720; greatest American theologian; leader of the Great Awakening; delivered "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" (1741); President of Princeton; grandfather of Aaron Burr Jr. (3rd U.S. Vice President). Father's father-in-law was Rev. Timothy Edwards who held one enslaved person named Ansars. Edwards himself enslaved at least six people: Venus (purchased 1731), Joab, Rose Binney, Titus (son of Joab and Rose), Joseph, Sue, and possibly Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Venus
enslaved_persons.description: Purchased June 1731 as a young teenager. Likely later baptized and given name Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Titus
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Son of Joab and Rose.

enslaved_persons.name: Joseph
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Married couple with Sue.

cross_connections.display_label: Giles Hooker Cowles studied theology under him after graduating Yale in 1789.
Succeeded his son-in-law as Princeton president after his sudden death.
Hosted him in final illness; published his Life as bestseller
Physician-minister and intimate friend in his New York years

education.notes: Entered 1716 at age 13. Graduated with highest honors.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1741 sermon shocked New England
narrative_blocks.body: On July 8, 1741, he delivered 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God' to a Connecticut congregation. The Great Awakening had been spreading from his Northampton parish for six years.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote his greatest works in frontier exile
narrative_blocks.body: After Northampton he took the Stockbridge mission preaching to Housatonnuck families through an interpreter. There he composed Freedom of the Will, The Nature of True Virtue, and The Doctrine of Original Sin.

narrative_blocks.header: Town turned on him over a midwifery book
narrative_blocks.body: In 1748 he disciplined young people in his Northampton parish for circulating what he called 'immoral books,' later identified as a midwifery manual. His own Williams and Hawley relatives led the council that dismissed him in 1750.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty-five days into the Princeton presidency
narrative_blocks.body: He accepted the College of New Jersey presidency in early 1758 after his son-in-law Aaron Burr Sr. died suddenly in office. A smallpox inoculation administered February 23 turned fatal on March 22.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale valedictorian at sixteen, in Latin
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale at twelve and graduated four years later as the youngest member of his class. He delivered the commencement valedictory address in Latin.

sources.short: Life of Rev. Jonathan Edwards by Sereno Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Princeton Cemetery, Princeton, New Jersey — buried next to Jonathan Edwards

cross_connections.display_label: Died trying to bring him home from Princeton at age two.
Died trying to bring her home from Princeton at age four.
Her North Haven nephew — wealthiest man in town in 1786

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Pierpont Edwards (1710-1758), wife of Jonathan Edwards.
bio.bio_blurb: Subject of Edwards’s famous meditation at thirteen

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards wrote about her when she was twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Edwards composed a private spiritual meditation about a 'young lady in New Haven who is beloved of that Great Being who made and rules the world.' She married him five years later, at seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought home her orphaned Burr grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: After Jonathan's death she traveled south in autumn 1758 to retrieve four-year-old Sarah Burr and two-year-old Aaron Burr Jr. She fell sick with dysentery in Philadelphia before she could complete the journey.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly six months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan died of smallpox-inoculation complications on March 22, 1758, weeks into the Princeton presidency. Sarah died of dysentery on October 2, 1758, at forty-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Made her will the day she died
narrative_blocks.body: On October 2, 1758, in Philadelphia, she dictated her last instructions hours before dysentery took her. The two Burr grandchildren she had been escorting were placed with Dr. William Shippen.

narrative_blocks.header: Her portrait hangs in a Yale dining hall
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband were both painted in oils, traditionally attributed to John Smybert. The portraits were bequeathed to Yale in 1939 and now hang in Jonathan Edwards College.
